meta.title: Ancient Astronomy & EO: Saving the Appearances

current_revision.content:
Saving the Appearances

Sōzein ta phainomena (σῴζειν τὰ φαινόμενα) — saving the appearances — is the demand, originating in ancient Greek astronomical practice, that a theory must be faithful to the phenomena: it must contain every observation without discarding, overriding, or distorting any of them. The term does not mean "explain the appearances" or "prove the appearances." It means: do not lose them.

Emergent Ontology adopts this as its first and prior standard. Before EO can be evaluated as an account of what transformation truly is, it must meet the Ptolemaic standard: the nine operators and the 27-cell capacity ground must be able to receive every described transformation in every domain — without remainder, without forcing the description into a shape it doesn't have, and without requiring that some appearances be set aside until later.

This article describes the standard, why it is prior to explanatory claims, the current state of change-description that makes it necessary, and what it means in practice for EO's development.

1. The Standard

The phrase sōzein ta phainomena entered astronomical discourse as a description of what mathematical models must do. A model that predicts some motions but requires you to ignore others has not saved the appearances — it has selected them. The demand is for completeness, not correctness: a system that preserves all the observations intact, even before it can say why they are that way.

Ptolemy's Almagest — the Mathēmatikē Syntaxis — is the greatest worked example of this standard in the history of science. Its mechanism: deferents and epicycles, equants and eccentrics, a layered geometry of circular motions composable into any observed planetary path. The Ptolemaic system was not ontologically correct. The planets do not actually move on circles. But it could contain every observed planetary position with extraordinary precision, and it did so for over fourteen centuries, unsurpassed in predictive power until Kepler and Newton.

What the Ptolemaic system demonstrated:

  • Adequacy of the grammar precedes truth of the ontology. You do not need to know why Mars traces the path it traces. You need a notation sufficient to describe the path. Only once the description is adequate can the question of causes be meaningfully posed.

  • Completeness is the harder and earlier demand. A system that explains five planets correctly and cannot account for a sixth has failed the prior test, regardless of the beauty of its causal account for the five.

  • The standard is falsifiable. An appearance that cannot be assigned an address in the notation — that falls outside what the grammar can say — is an empirical challenge to the system's adequacy, not a philosophical objection to its ontology.

Thomas Aquinas described the epistemic status precisely: a theory like the Ptolemaic system "is established because thereby the sensible appearances of the heavenly movements can be explained — not, however, as if this proof were sufficient, forasmuch as some other theory might explain them." Adequacy is not uniqueness. Saving the appearances does not close the question of what the appearances mean.

2. Where We Stand: The Pre-Ptolemaic State of Change Description

For planetary astronomy, Ptolemy had appearances to save. Centuries of Babylonian and Greek observation had regularised the celestial motions into a corpus: positions, periods, retrograde arcs, occultations. The appearances existed as a shared, cross-cultural, translatable record before any theoretical account of them.

It is worth being precise about what that corpus actually was. The Babylonians were not cataloguing stars as things — not their substance, their divine significance, their physical composition. They were cataloguing relational positions over time: angular separations, conjunction periods, retrograde arcs, how each body moved relative to every other body as the record accumulated across generations. The appearances they preserved were fundamentally relational and temporal — not objects but patterns of relation between objects as they changed.

This sharpens what Ptolemy inherited. He did not receive a list of celestial objects. He received a corpus of how things stood in transformation relative to each other. The grammar he built — epicycles, deferents, equants — was a notation for saving that relational-temporal record, not for describing what planets are.

The equivalent for EO is therefore more specific than "a list of transformations." What is needed is a corpus of relational dependencies between transformation types: what precedes what, what depends on what, what produces what, what cannot occur without what. The helix dependency ordering is the proposed theory. The corpus that would test it — the equivalent of the Babylonian clay tablets — is a systematic cross-domain record of those dependencies.

EO has partial versions of this corpus: the cross-linguistic verb distribution (which records the relative frequency and co-occurrence patterns of transformation types across 27 languages), the cross-cultural crosswalk (which records independent convergences on the same relational structures across 40+ traditions), the relational algebra mapping (which records dependency constraints in a formal system). None of these is yet the full observational record that the theory requires. The tablets are incomplete.

No equivalent corpus exists for transformation and change at the scale the Ptolemaic analogy demands.

What exists instead is a collection of isolated, domain-specific notations, each adequate within its own domain and mutually unintelligible across domains:

  • Differential equations (physics): adequate for continuous motion, rates of change, dynamical systems. Cannot say what kind of change is occurring — only how fast.

  • State machines and process calculi (computation): adequate for discrete transitions between defined states. Cannot represent the change in the system that generates states.

  • Aspect systems in linguistics: adequate for grammatical distinctions between perfective, imperfective, inchoative, and completive events. Untranslatable into physical or computational notation.

  • Process ontologies (Whitehead, BFO occurrents): adequate for philosophical description of temporal extension. No shared vocabulary with operational or linguistic accounts.

  • Developmental stage models (psychology, organisational theory): adequate for describing transformations in cognitive or social systems. Incompatible with process-calculus descriptions of the same phenomena.

The consequence is precise: a phase transition, a perceptual pop-out, a grammatical aspect shift, a species-level evolutionary event, a conceptual revolution — all are transformations, but the disciplines that study them have no shared grammar for what kind of transformation each is. They cannot compare structural forms. They cannot ask whether a social upheaval has the same transformation type as a thermodynamic phase change. The question cannot even be posed, because the notation for posing it does not exist.

This is the pre-Ptolemaic condition. Not that we lack good theories of change within domains — we have many. The deficit is that we lack the prior thing: a grammar of appearance for transformation that operates across domains without domain-specific encoding. We are not even at the stage of the Babylonian observation logs — because the Babylonians at least shared a single sky. The domains that study transformation do not share an observational field. Their notations are not only untranslatable; they do not agree on what counts as an observation. The appearances have not yet been saved, and the corpus required to save them does not yet fully exist.

3. The Ptolemaic Claim EO Must Establish

EO's first and prior claim — prior to any ontological claim about what transformation is — is:

The nine operators and the 27-cell capacity ground constitute a grammar sufficient to assign a structural address to any described transformation in any domain, without remainder and without distortion.

This is the Ptolemaic claim. It is not the claim that EO's operators describe the actual causal structure of transformation. It is not the claim that the triadic axes track real ontological strata. It is not the claim that the helix dependency ordering is discovered rather than constructed. Those are the Newtonian questions — they come after, and they require the Ptolemaic foundation to be solid first.

The Ptolemaic claim is strictly about expressive completeness. Can every transformation anyone points to receive an address? Can every appearance be saved without loss?

What the claim requires:

A description of a transformation — in any domain, in any tradition, in any notation — must be expressible using the nine operators as a vocabulary, producing an address in the 27-cell capacity ground. The address must:

  1. Assign the transformation to one or more operator positions without forcing it into a shape it does not have.

  2. Not require the description to be trimmed, simplified, or corrected before it can be addressed.

  3. Not leave a remainder — a component of the description that has no address in the grammar.

What the claim does not require:

The Ptolemaic claim does not require that EO's address is the only correct address, or that different analysts would assign the same address, or that the address reveals anything about why the transformation has the structure it does. Ptolemy's epicycle calculation for Mars was not the only way to describe Mars's path. It was a way that worked. That is all the standard demands at this stage.

4. The Residue Test

The correct adversarial stress test for EO at this stage is not "is EO's ontology true?" It is: does EO have the expressive completeness to contain the appearances?

The test procedure:

  1. Take transformation descriptions from domains EO was not designed for: immunological response, jazz improvisation, tectonic subduction, grief, ecological succession, the development of a legal norm, the emergence of a new literary genre.

  2. Attempt to assign each a structural address using the nine operators and the capacity ground.

  3. Identify the residue: anything in the description that cannot be assigned an address without distortion.

Residue is the empirical challenge. If a class of transformations consistently produces remainder — something in the description that falls outside what the grammar can say — that is a genuine falsification of the Ptolemaic claim, and it identifies the direction in which the grammar needs to be extended or revised.

Two outcomes are not residue and should not be treated as such:

  • Underdetermination: multiple valid addresses for the same description. The Ptolemaic system offered multiple epicycle configurations for the same planetary path. Underdetermination means the grammar has more expressive power than any single description requires — which is not a failure.

  • Empty cells: the grammar contains addresses to which no known transformation has been assigned. The empty cell at SYN × Ground — the position that has returned zero verbs across 32,000+ verbs in 27 languages and zero transformations across every domain tested — is not a gap in the coverage of appearances. It is itself an appearance: the structural observation that a particular kind of transformation is either impossible or radically impoverished in human practice. Saving this appearance means preserving the void as data, not filling it in.

5. The Ptolemaic Claim and the Newtonian Claim

The history of astronomy distinguishes two moments:

The Ptolemaic moment: a grammar adequate to the appearances. Predictively powerful, faithful to observation, expressively complete — but silent on causes. The Almagest does not explain why planets move. It describes how they appear to move with sufficient precision that the description can be trusted for navigation, calendrics, and prediction.

The Newtonian moment: a causal account that explains why the appearances are what they are. Newton's law of universal gravitation does not merely predict planetary positions — it explains why planets follow the paths they do, derives Kepler's empirical laws from first principles, and unifies celestial and terrestrial mechanics under a single causal framework. The Ptolemaic grammar becomes a special case of the Newtonian account.

For EO, these map onto distinct claims at different stages of development:

Stage

Claim

Falsification

Ptolemaic

The nine operators constitute a grammar sufficient to address any described transformation

A transformation description that produces genuine remainder in the grammar

Newtonian

The nine operators and their dependency ordering reflect the actual causal structure of transformation

A case where the dependency ordering is shown to be contingent or domain-specific rather than structural

EO currently has strong evidence bearing on the Ptolemaic claim — the cross-linguistic verb corpus, the cross-cultural crosswalk, the relational algebra mapping, the developmental biology correspondence — but this evidence is not yet consolidated into a systematic residue test. The Newtonian claim has not been formally attempted and may not be the right frame: whether transformation has "causes" in the Newtonian sense is itself an open question.

The development priority is clear: the Ptolemaic claim must be established — or its failures identified — before the Newtonian claim can be meaningfully posed.

6. Why This Standard Is Prior to Proof

The point is not that EO is modest. The point is that the demand for adequacy-of-appearance precedes the demand for truth-of-explanation in any rigorous scientific programme.

Asking whether EO is "true" before it has demonstrated expressive completeness is asking the wrong question at the wrong stage. It is asking Newton's question before Ptolemy's work is done. The question "are the nine operators the real structure of change?" cannot be evaluated until we know that the nine operators can say everything that needs to be said about how change appears. If the grammar is incomplete — if there are genuine appearances it cannot contain — then the ontological question is moot: the grammar is inadequate before it is true or false.

Conversely: a grammar that saves all the appearances has earned the right to be taken seriously as an ontological claim, even if it has not yet demonstrated that its structural account is causally correct. Ptolemy earned fourteen centuries of authority not by proving heliocentrism wrong but by producing a system adequate to the phenomena. That adequacy was real and consequential work. It had to be done before Copernicus, Kepler, and Newton could do theirs.

EO's situation is precisely this. The current fragmentation of change-description across incompatible domain notations is not a theoretical problem — it is a pre-theoretical one. The grammar for saving the appearances of transformation does not yet exist as a shared, cross-domain vocabulary. That grammar is what EO proposes to be. Whether the proposal succeeds is a testable question. But the test is adequacy first, truth second.

7. Relation to Other EO Articles

The Ptolemaic standard connects to several structural features of EO discussed elsewhere:

  • Bivalent Compression and Dimensional Poverty: the fragmentation of change-description across incompatible notations is a dimensional-compression problem — each notation operates on one or two faces of the capacity ground without access to the full three-dimensional structure. A shared grammar for transformation appearances requires the full 27-cell space.

  • The Empty Cell (SYN × Ground): the most important single piece of evidence bearing on EO's Ptolemaic claim. Its persistent absence across all tested domains is itself an appearance — one that a complete grammar of transformation must be able to contain and name, not explain away.

  • Cross-Linguistic Findings: the 32,000-verb corpus is the largest systematic attempt to test whether the nine operators constitute a universal vocabulary for transformation appearances in natural language. Its findings are the closest thing EO currently has to a Ptolemaic observational corpus.

  • The Three Faces: any adequate grammar of transformation must be simultaneously adequate on the Act face (what operation is occurring), the Site face (what kind of thing is being transformed), and the Resolution face (at what grain the transformation engages). Adequacy on one face with gaps on another is not adequacy.

  • Influences and Lineage — Wilson: Wilson's consilience project is the methodological precedent. EO's cross-domain ambition is not a claim that all domains are the same — it is the prior claim that they are all subject to the same constraints on coherent transformation, and that those constraints generate a shared grammar of appearance.
